title: Conjunctions
description: Connect words, phrases, and clauses using coordinating, subordinating, and correlative conjunctions.

forms.positive: Coordinating (FANBOYS: for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so) | Subordinating (because, although, when, if, since) | Correlative (either...or, neither...nor, not only...but also)
forms.question: Do the clauses joined have equal weight (coordinating) or does one depend on the other (subordinating)?

usages.description: Coordinating conjunctions link two independent clauses or grammatically equal elements. Remember the acronym FANBOYS: For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So.
examples: I wanted to go outside, **but** it was raining.
She is tired, **yet** she keeps working.

usages.description: Subordinating conjunctions begin a dependent clause, connecting it to the main independent clause and showing relationships like cause, contrast, time, or condition.
examples: He passed the exam **because** he studied every day.
**Although** she was nervous, she gave a great speech.
We will leave **when** you are ready.

usages.description: Correlative conjunctions come in pairs and link equivalent grammatical elements with a clear relationship.
examples: She is **not only** kind **but also** very patient.
**Neither** the manager **nor** the staff knew about the issue.

commonMistakes.incorrect: I was tired but however I finished the work.
commonMistakes.correct: I was tired, but I finished the work. / I was tired; however, I finished the work.
commonMistakes.explanation: 'But' and 'however' both express contrast. Using them together is redundant. Use one or the other — 'but' as a coordinating conjunction or 'however' as a transitional adverb with a semicolon.

commonMistakes.incorrect: Because she studied hard, so she passed.
commonMistakes.correct: Because she studied hard, she passed. / She studied hard, so she passed.
commonMistakes.explanation: 'Because' and 'so' cannot both be used in the same sentence. One clause must be the cause and the other the effect — pick one conjunction.

quizzes.question: Which type of conjunction links these two clauses? 'She is smart, but she lacks confidence.'
quizzes.explanation: 'But' is one of the FANBOYS coordinating conjunctions, linking two independent clauses of equal weight.

quizzes.question: Choose the correct conjunction: 'We cancelled the trip _______ the weather was dangerous.'
quizzes.explanation: 'Because' is a subordinating conjunction showing cause — the dangerous weather caused the trip cancellation.

quizzes.question: Complete the correlative pair: 'Neither the CEO _______ the board approved the plan.'
quizzes.explanation: 'Neither...nor' is the correct correlative conjunction pair. 'Neither...or' is grammatically incorrect.
